questions.question: What is a Binary Tree?

options.A: A tree with nodes having only two children
options.B: A tree with nodes having only one parent
options.C: A tree with nodes having no children
options.D: A tree with nodes having no parents

questions.explanation: A binary tree is a data structure in which each node has at most two children (left child and right child). This characteristic makes it useful for many applications, such as searching and sorting.

questions.question: What is the term for the order in which the left and right subtrees are traversed in a pre-order traversal of a binary tree?
questions.explanation: In a pre-order traversal, the root node is visited first, followed by the left subtree, and then the right subtree. This order is denoted as 'root -> left -> right'.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting a node in the middle of a balanced binary search tree?
questions.explanation: In a balanced binary search tree, the time complexity of inserting a node is O(log n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree. This is because the tree remains self-balancing, ensuring that the height of the tree remains relatively small.

questions.question: Can a binary search tree contain duplicate values?

options.A: Yes, as values are stored in nodes
options.B: No, as duplicate values can cause inconsistencies
options.C: Only if the tree is not self-balancing
options.D: Only if the tree is degenerate

questions.explanation: Although binary search trees typically store unique values, some implementations can store duplicate values in nodes. However, it's essential to handle duplicates carefully to maintain the tree's integrity.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the NULL child pointer in a binary tree node?

options.B: To indicate the absence of children

questions.explanation: The NULL child pointer in a binary tree node typically represents the absence of a child node. This allows for efficient memory allocation and node structure.

questions.question: What is a full binary tree?

options.A: A tree where all nodes have at most one child
options.B: A tree where every node has two children
options.C: A tree where all nodes except the leaves have two children
options.D: A tree where all nodes are leaves

questions.explanation: A full binary tree is a tree where every node, except for the leaves, has two children. This definition ensures that every internal node has a complete binary subtree.

questions.question: How does a binary tree handle left and right rotations?

options.A: Left rotation: the node with the smaller value moves to its right child
options.B: Right rotation: the node with the smaller value moves to its left child
options.C: Left rotation: the node with the larger value moves to its left child
options.D: Right rotation: the node with the larger value moves to its right child

questions.explanation: In a binary tree, a left rotation occurs when a node with a larger value moves to its left child, while a right rotation occurs when a node with a smaller value moves to its right child.

questions.question: What is the difference between an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) and a Binary Tree?

options.A: ASTs store values, whereas Binary Trees store nodes
options.B: Binary Trees are used for parsing, whereas ASTs are used for code evaluation
options.C: Binary Trees represent a hierarchical structure, whereas ASTs represent a parse tree
options.D: ASTs are a specific type of binary tree

questions.explanation: While both abstract syntax trees (ASTs) and binary trees can represent hierarchical structures, ASTs specifically represent the syntax of a programming language, making them a distinct data structure.

questions.question: Can a degenerate binary tree be considered a valid binary tree?

options.A: No, as its structure is not hierarchical
options.B: Yes, if it has at least one node
options.C: Yes, if it has a valid root node

questions.explanation: A degenerate binary tree can be considered a valid binary tree if it has at least one node. The absence of nodes other than the root still satisfies the basic definition of a binary tree.

questions.question: What is the average case time complexity of inserting a new node into an n-node binary search tree?
questions.explanation: In an n-node binary search tree, the average case time complexity of inserting a new node is O(log n), as the tree is self-balancing, ensuring that the tree's height remains relatively small.

questions.question: How do you represent a null node in a binary tree?

options.D: Using extra memory for each node

questions.explanation: A null node in a binary tree is often represented by a special 'nil' sentinel, which helps indicate the absence of a child node.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a sentinel node in a binary tree?
questions.explanation: A sentinel node in a binary tree is used to represent a null or missing child node, simplifying the tree's structure and enabling more efficient node creation.

questions.question: When traversing a binary tree using a depth-first search, what happens when a node has no left child?
questions.explanation: In a depth-first search traversal, when a node has no left child, the algorithm typically traverses the right subtree first. The left subtree is only explored if it becomes relevant at a later point.

questions.question: What is the term for a binary tree with all levels completely filled, except possibly the last level?

options.D: Deepest node in a full subtree

questions.explanation: A complete binary tree is a binary tree with all levels completely filled, except possibly the last level. All nodes in the last level, if any, are filled from left to right.
